---
title: "The January Garden"
description: "January strips the garden back to its bones. What remains is, in its own way, beautiful."
author: "Sophia Jaffer"
date: "2025-01-01"
categories:
  - Seasons
  - Garden
---

## Bare

January is the month when the garden tells the truth. The soft abundance of summer — the screens of leaf, the draping of flower over fence — has gone, and what is left is structure: the bare fork of the apple tree, the dark ribs of the rose, the skeleton of the climbing hydrangea pressed flat against the wall.

I find I don't mind it. There is something clarifying about this kind of exposure. You see the shape of things. You understand, for the first time in months, where everything actually stands.

## What survives

The hellebores are beginning. They hang their heads in the way they do — as if embarrassed by their own persistence — and I have to crouch to see their faces properly. They are an old, dark red, almost the colour of dried blood, which sounds grim but isn't. They are reliable in a way that summer flowers are not. They turn up regardless.

The birds are more visible now too. A blackbird has claimed the compost heap as his territory. He stands on top of it in the mornings and sings with what seems like unnecessary ambition for such a grey hour.

## Attention

I think gardening in winter is really a practice in paying attention to small things. There is nothing dramatic to report — no sudden flush of bloom, no riot of scent. But there is always something: a new shoot at the base of the fig, the first tight bud on the forsythia, the way frost holds on the north-facing slope of the lawn until noon.

It is enough. It is more than enough, if you are willing to look closely.
